title: भगवद्गीता ध्यान श्लोक
devanagariText:
ॐ पार्थाय प्रतिबोधितां भगवता नारायणेन स्वयं
व्यासेन ग्रथितां पुराणमुनिना मध्ये महाभारतम्।
अद्वैतामृतवर्षिणीं भगवतीमष्टादशाध्यायिनीं
अम्ब त्वामनुसन्दधामि भगवद्गीते भवद्वेषिणीम्॥१॥

नमोऽस्तु ते व्यास विशालबुद्धे
फुल्लारविन्दायतपत्रनेत्र।
येन त्वया भारततैलपूर्णः
प्रज्वालितो ज्ञानमयः प्रदीपः॥२॥

प्रपन्नपारिजाताय तोत्रवेत्रैकपाणये।
ज्ञानमुद्राय कृष्णाय गीतामृतदुहे नमः॥३॥

सर्वोपनिषदो गावो दोग्धा गोपालनन्दनः।
पार्थो वत्सः सुधीर्भोक्ता दुग्धं गीतामृतं महत्॥४॥

वसुदेवसुतं देवं कंसचाणूरमर्दनम्।
देवकीपरमानन्दं कृष्णं वन्दे जगद्गुरुम्॥५॥

भीष्मद्रोणतटा जयद्रथजला गान्धारनीलोत्पला
शल्यग्राहवती कृपेण वहनी कर्णेन वेलाकुला।
अश्वत्थामविकर्णघोरमकरा दुर्योधनावर्तिनी
सोत्तीर्णा खलु पाण्डवै रणनदी कैवर्तकः केशवः॥६॥

पाराशर्यवचः सरोजममलं गीतार्थगन्धोत्कटं
नानाख्यानककेसरं हरिकथासम्बोधनाबोधितम्।
लोके सज्जनषट्पदैरहरहः पेपीयमानं मुदा
भूयाद्भारतपङ्कजं कलिमलप्रध्वंसि नः श्रेयसे॥७॥

मूकं करोति वाचालं पङ्गुं लङ्घयते गिरिम्।
यत्कृपा तमहं वन्दे परमानन्दमाधवम्॥८॥

यं ब्रह्मा वरुणेन्द्ररुद्रमरुतः स्तुन्वन्ति दिव्यैः स्तवैः
वेदैः साङ्गपदक्रमोपनिषदैर्गायन्ति यं सामगाः।
ध्यानावस्थिततद्गतेन मनसा पश्यन्ति यं योगिनो
यस्यान्तं न विदुः सुरासुरगणा देवाय तस्मै नमः॥९॥
transliteration:
Om Parthaya Pratibodhitam Bhagavata Narayanena Svayam
Vyasena Grathitam Purana-Munina Madhye Mahabharatam.
Advaita-Amrita-Varshinim Bhagavatim-Ashtadasha-Adhyayinim
Amba Tvam-Anusandadhami Bhagavad-Gite Bhava-Dveshinim.||1||

Namo-Astu Te Vyasa Vishala-Buddhe
Phulla-Aravinda-Ayata-Patra-Netra.
Yena Tvaya Bharata-Taila-Purnah
Prajvalito Jnanamayah Pradipah.||2||

Prapanna-Parijataya Totra-Vetraika-Panaye.
Jnana-Mudraya Krishnaya Gita-Amrita-Duhe Namah.||3||

Sarva-Upanishado Gavo Dogdha Gopala-Nandanah.
Partho Vatsah Sudhir-Bhokta Dugdham Gita-Amritam Mahat.||4||

Vasudevasutam Devam Kamsa-Chanura-Mardanam.
Devaki-Paramanandam Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum.||5||

Bhishma-Drona-Tata Jayadratha-Jala Gandhari-Nilotpala
Shalya-Grahavati Kripena Vahani Karnena Vela-Akula.
Ashvatthama-Vikarna-Ghora-Makara Duryodhana-Avartini
Sottirna Khalu Pandavai Rana-Nadi Kaivartakah Keshavah.||6||

Parasharya-Vachah Sarojam-Amalam Gita-Artha-Gandhotkhatam
Nana-Akhyanaka-Kesaram Hari-Katha-Sambodhana-Abodhitam.
Loke Sajjana-Shat-Padair-Ahara-Hah Pepiyamanam Muda
Bhuyad-Bharata-Pankajam Kali-Mala-Pradhvamsi Nah Shreyase.||7||

Mukam Karoti Vachalam Pangum Langhayate Girim.
Yat-Kripa Tam-Aham Vande Paramananda-Madhavam.||8||

Yam Brahma Varuna-Indra-Rudra-Marutah Stunvanti Divyaih Stavaih
Vedaih Sa-Anga-Pada-Krama-Upanishadair-Gayanti Yam Samagah.
Dhyana-Avasthita-Tad-Gatena Manasa Pashyanti Yam Yogino
Yasya-Antam Na Viduh Sura-Asura-Ganah Devaya Tasmai Namah.||9||
hindiMeaning:
हे भगवद्गीता माँ! जो भगवान नारायण द्वारा स्वयं अर्जुन को बोधित की गई, पुराणमुनि व्यास द्वारा महाभारत के मध्य में ग्रथित की गई, जो अद्वैत-अमृत की वर्षा करने वाली, अठारह अध्यायों वाली, भव (संसार) को नष्ट करने वाली हैं - हे अम्बा! मैं आपका ध्यान करता हूँ।

हे विशाल बुद्धि वाले व्यास! आपको नमस्कार। जिनके नेत्र खिले हुए कमल के पत्र के समान विशाल हैं, जिन्होंने भारत (महाभारत) रूपी तेल से भरे ज्ञानमय दीपक को प्रज्वलित किया।

सम्पूर्ण उपनिषद गौएँ हैं, गोपाल-नन्दन (श्रीकृष्ण) दुहने वाले हैं, अर्जुन बछड़ा है, बुद्धिमान लोग भोक्ता हैं, और गीतामृत वह महान दुग्ध है।

जिनकी कृपा से गूँगा वाचाल हो जाता है और लंगड़ा पर्वत लाँघ जाता है, उन परमानन्द माधव को मैं वन्दन करता हूँ।
benefits: Traditionally invoked to establish the perfect meditative state before gita study
Invokes the blessings of Vyasa and Krishna
Traditionally recited for removal of ignorance and grants spiritual wisdom
Purifies the mind for receiving divine knowledge
Traditionally recited for removal of sins accumulated in kali yuga
Essential preparation for Bhagavad Gita recitation
description: Bhagavad Gita Dhyana Shloka (भगवद्गीता ध्यान श्लोक) is a sacred prayer dedicated to Lord Krishna. Rooted in Gita Mahatmya (Padma Purana), this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Regular recitation is believed to create the perfect meditative state before Gita study, purify the mind for receiving divine knowledge, and destroy sins accumulated in Kali Yuga. This sacred text is ideally recited during Janmashtami for enhanced blessings. Comprising 9 verses, it can be completed in approximately 7 minutes, making it suitable for daily devotional practice. On this page, you can read the complete Bhagavad Gita Dhyana Shloka in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.

seoFaqs.question: What is the Bhagavad Gita Dhyana Shloka?
seoFaqs.answer: The Bhagavad Gita Dhyana Shloka (भगवद्गीता ध्यान श्लोक — Gita Dhyanam) is a sacred 9-verse Sanskrit invocation traditionally chanted as the meditative preface (dhyana) before reciting the Bhagavad Gita itself. It is preserved in classical Gita parayana paddhati and is universally chanted across all sampradayas — Advaita, Vishishtadvaita, Dvaita, Gaudiya, Pushti Marg and Warkari — at the start of every Gita study, Gita Saptaha (seven-day Gita parayana), and Gita Jayanti recitation. The dhyanam opens with the famous invocation 'Om Parthaya Pratibodhitam Bhagavata Narayanena Svayam' (Om — to Him who was personally awakened to right knowledge by Bhagavan Narayana for the sake of Partha/Arjuna), praises Vyasa for compiling the Mahabharata, salutes the Gita as the milk drawn by Krishna the cowherd from the Upanishadic cow, and invokes the dhyanam-figures of Madhusudana, Krishna and the Pandava-Kaurava battlefield — preparing the heart and mind for the supreme dialogue. (Source: Gita Mahatmya tradition; classical Gita parayana paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of 'Om Parthaya Pratibodhitam' the opening verse of Gita Dhyana?
seoFaqs.answer: The opening verse of the Gita Dhyana — Om Parthaya Pratibodhitam Bhagavata Narayanena Svayam / Vyasena Grathitam Purana-Munina Madhye Mahabharatam / Advaitamrita-Varshinim Bhagavatim Ashtadasha-Adhyayinim / Amba Tvam Anusandadhami Bhagavad-Gite Bhava-Dveshinim — translates as: "O Mother Bhagavad-Gita, you who were taught by Bhagavan Narayana Himself for the awakening of Partha (Arjuna), who were strung together in the middle of the Mahabharata by Vyasa the ancient sage, who shower the nectar of Advaita, who are divine and consist of eighteen chapters, and who destroy the fear of repeated birth — I contemplate you again and again." This single verse compresses the entire Gita's authorship (Krishna), its scribe (Vyasa), its location (Mahabharata Bhishma Parva), its philosophical fruit (Advaita-amrita), its structure (eighteen chapters), and its purpose (liberation from samsara) — addressing the Gita reverentially as Mother (Amba). (Source: Gita Dhyanam verse 1.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the famous cowherd-cow-milk metaphor in Gita Dhyana Shloka?
seoFaqs.answer: Gita Dhyana Shloka verse 4 contains one of the most beloved metaphors in Hindu literature: sarvopanishado gavo dogdha gopala-nandanah / partho vatsah sudhir bhokta dugdham gita-amritam mahat — "All the Upanishads are the cows; the Cowherd-Boy (Krishna) is the milker; Partha (Arjuna) is the calf; the wise are the drinkers; and the great Gita-nectar is the milk drawn." The image is exquisite: just as a cowherd makes the cow yield milk by allowing the calf to suckle, Krishna drew the supreme nectar of the Upanishads from the Vedic cows by using the calf — Arjuna's questions — as the device that prompts the milk to flow. The result is the great Gita-amrita that all wise people drink. This verse establishes the Gita as a complete Upanishadic essence — and is the source of the traditional title Gita-Upanishad applied to the Bhagavad Gita's chapter colophons. (Source: Gita Dhyanam verse 4; classical Vedanta tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: When is the Bhagavad Gita Dhyana Shloka traditionally recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The Bhagavad Gita Dhyana Shloka is traditionally recited on the following occasions: (1) at the very start of every Gita parayana — daily Gita reading, single-chapter recitation, or full eighteen-chapter parayana; (2) at the opening of Gita Saptaha (seven-day Gita reading) and Gita Pravachana (Gita discourse) sessions; (3) on Gita Jayanti (Margashirsha Shukla Ekadashi — the day Krishna spoke the Gita to Arjuna at Kurukshetra, celebrated each year in November-December); (4) at all Mahabharata parayana ceremonies; (5) before reading any single chapter (after which the relevant Mahatmya verses for that chapter are also recited); (6) at the start of Vedanta study (since the Gita is one of the three Prasthana-trayi alongside the Upanishads and Brahma Sutras); (7) at funeral and antyeshti ceremonies along with Gita Chapter 8 or 15. The dhyanam is the prescribed invocation across all sampradayas. (Source: classical Gita parayana paddhati; Drikpanchang Gita Jayanti.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the difference between Gita Dhyana Shloka and Gita Mahatmya?
seoFaqs.answer: Both are sacred invocations attached to the Bhagavad Gita parayana practice but differ in purpose, length and ritual placement. The Gita Dhyana Shloka is a 9-verse meditative invocation chanted at the very start of every Gita parayana session — it salutes Krishna, Vyasa, Arjuna, the Upanishadic cows, the Pandava-Kaurava battlefield, Madhusudana and Mother Gita herself, preparing the reciter's mind for the dialogue. The Gita Mahatmya is a longer composition (22-23 verses, preserved in the Padma Purana Uttara Khanda, Vaishnava Khanda) that praises the supreme glory and merit of reading each individual chapter — recited usually before each chapter so that the devotee knows the specific phala (fruit) of that chapter's parayana. There are also chapter-specific Mahatmyas (Adhyaya-Mahatmya) for each of the eighteen chapters. The Dhyanam invokes; the Mahatmya glorifies. Both are recited together in formal parayana. (Source: classical Gita parayana paddhati; Padma Purana Gita Mahatmya.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does chanting the Gita Dhyana Shloka grant?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition attributes the following benefits to chanting the Gita Dhyana Shloka before Gita parayana: (1) Sankalpa-shuddhi — purification of intent, ensuring that the parayana is undertaken with the right meditative attitude rather than mere ritual habit; (2) chitta-ekagrata — single-pointed concentration, since the dhyanam visualizes Krishna teaching Arjuna on the Kurukshetra battlefield, anchoring the reciter in the dramatic context; (3) Vishnu-Krishna-anugraha — the grace of Krishna invoked through the salutation Mukundam Madhavam Govindam (verse 8), ensuring that the words of the Gita penetrate the heart; (4) Vyasa-prasada — the blessing of sage Vyasa, the compiler, ensuring correct understanding; (5) removal of vighnas (obstacles) that may interrupt the parayana; (6) connection to the unbroken parampara (lineage) of Gita teachers and devotees from Krishna onwards. The dhyanam is the mantra-pushpa (mantra-flower) offered to Mother Gita before drinking her nectar. (Source: traditional Gita parayana paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: Who composed the Bhagavad Gita Dhyana Shloka?
seoFaqs.answer: The Bhagavad Gita Dhyana Shloka has been preserved in the classical Gita parayana tradition for many centuries; the precise authorship of the 9-verse compilation is not unanimously attributed to a single named composer in scholarly tradition. Some Vedanta scholars associate the famous opening 'Om Parthaya' verse and the cowherd-cow milk verse with Madhusudana Saraswati (16th-century Advaita acharya) who included these verses in his Gudhartha Dipika commentary; other scholars place them earlier, in the Gita Mahatmya tradition of the Padma Purana corpus. The verses are universally accepted across all sampradayas without sectarian dispute, suggesting an ancient and pre-sectarian origin. What is certain is that by the time of the major medieval Gita commentaries, the dhyanam was already established as the standard pre-parayana invocation, and it has been chanted continuously by Hindu devotees ever since — making it part of the living Sanatana parampara regardless of any single named author. (Source: classical Gita parayana tradition; Madhusudana Saraswati's Gudhartha Dipika.)
